sibling_claims.P1: Schizophrenia is not marked off from ordinary life by the presence of hallucinations or strange beliefs — those are common — but by their persistence, conviction, and cost; the diagnostic line is real, yet it is a threshold on a continuum, not a wall.
sibling_claims.P3: The 'split mind' folk-model is the exact inverse of the mechanism — this is a failure of integration, not a multiplication of selves.
sibling_claims.P4: The most powerful things that help in schizophrenia are the least-discussed — coordinated care and family support rival medication for preventing relapse — and 'non-adherence' is usually a rational response to real side-effect burden, not a failure of will.
sibling_claims.P5: Living well with schizophrenia is governed less by the illness itself than by things the word 'support' usually ignores — a body that dies about fifteen years early of preventable heart disease, a world that treats the victim as the threat, and a recovery rate real enough to matter and contested enough that no single number is honest.

sources.title: Silencing the self: schizophrenia as a self-disturbance.
sources.finding: Published Schizophrenia Bulletin first-person account describing psychosis as disintegration of the self and loss of ownership of one's own thoughts, emotions and actions (distinct from Chadwick 2007 / Gardiner 2021).
sources.verbatim_quote: I was simply sectioned again, detached from my real self, observing what was being done to me in a third-person perspective. ... My thoughts, my emotions, and my actions, none of them belong to me any more.

sources.title: Night's end.
sources.finding: Second real Schizophrenia Bulletin first-person account (Roberta Payne) with quotable auditory-hallucination descriptions; backup FPA.
sources.verbatim_quote: My brain created disc jockeys in the air vents of the hospital room playing Beatles' songs. ... I experienced extraordinarily loud fighter jets landing and taking off in my hospital room!

sources.title: Failure of hypothesis evaluation as a factor in delusional belief.
sources.finding: Mechanistic definition source: a delusion is characterized by maintenance of a belief despite disconfirming evidence (two-factor theory 'second factor').

numbers.what_it_is: definition of the defining feature of a delusion (survives counter-evidence)
numbers.abstract_sentence: In the two-factor theory of delusional belief, the second factor consists of a failure to reject hypotheses in the face of disconfirmatory evidence.

sources.title: Experiences of hearing voices: analysis of a novel phenomenological survey.
sources.finding: Largest phenomenological survey of voice-hearing (n=153): voices are perceptual, heterogeneous, and frequently NOT literally auditory. Sample mixed diagnoses incl. non-clinical — phenomenology, not schizophrenia-only prevalence.

numbers.what_it_is: proportion reporting thought-like or mixed (not literally auditory) voices
numbers.abstract_sentence: Less than half of the participants reported hearing literally auditory voices--70 (46%) individuals reported either thought-like or mixed experiences.

sources.title: The current conceptualization of negative symptoms in schizophrenia.
sources.finding: Consensus source defining five negative-symptom domains (blunted affect, alogia, anhedonia, asociality, avolition), their two-factor structure, and key role in functional outcome.

numbers.abstract_sentence: a) five constructs should be considered as negative symptoms, i.e. blunted affect, alogia, anhedonia, asociality and avolition; ... c) the five constructs cluster in two factors, one including blunted affect and alogia and the other consisting of anhedonia, avolition and asociality.

sources.title: The global cognitive impairment in schizophrenia: consistent over decades and around the world.
sources.finding: Meta-analysis (100 studies): generalized cognitive impairment of ~1 SD (grand mean g=-1.03), largest in processing speed and episodic memory; stable across decades and geography.

numbers.what_it_is: overall neurocognitive impairment vs healthy controls (~1 SD)
numbers.abstract_sentence: patients with schizophrenia scored significantly lower than controls across all cognitive tests and domains, grand mean effect size, g=-1.03.
Patients showed somewhat larger impairments in the domains of processing speed (g=-1.25) and episodic memory (g=-1.23).

sources.title: Neurocognitive deficit in schizophrenia: a quantitative review of the evidence.
sources.finding: Landmark meta-analysis (204 studies, 22 test variables): moderate-to-large deficits (d>.60) across every cognitive domain measured.

numbers.what_it_is: effect sizes across all 22 neurocognitive test variables
numbers.abstract_sentence: Moderate to large raw effect sizes (d > .60) were obtained for all 22 neurocognitive test variables, and none of the associated confidence intervals included zero.

sources.title: Overlooking the obvious: a meta-analytic comparison of digit symbol coding tasks and other cognitive measures in schizophrenia.
sources.finding: Meta-analysis (37 studies, 1961 patients): the single largest cognitive impairment is processing-speed/coding, g=-1.57.

numbers.what_it_is: effect size for digit symbol coding (processing-speed) deficit
numbers.abstract_sentence: The weighted mean effect for digit symbol coding was g = -1.57 (95% confidence interval, -1.66 to -1.48), significantly larger than effects for episodic memory, executive functioning, and working memory.

sources.title: What are the functional consequences of neurocognitive deficits in schizophrenia?
sources.finding: Landmark review: neurocognition (esp. verbal memory, vigilance) predicts real-world outcome, whereas positive symptoms did NOT predict outcome in any study reviewed — the load-bearing tension.

numbers.what_it_is: positive symptoms do not predict functional outcome; cognition does
numbers.abstract_sentence: psychotic symptoms were not significantly associated with outcome measures in any of the studies reviewed.

sources.title: Negative symptoms have greater impact on functioning than positive symptoms in schizophrenia: analysis of CATIE data.
sources.finding: CATIE analysis (n=1447): functioning was more strongly related to negative symptoms than any other domain, including positive — with honest caveat that overall variance explained was small.

numbers.what_it_is: CATIE sample; negative symptoms most related to functioning
numbers.abstract_sentence: Baseline functioning and change in functioning were more strongly related to PANSS negative factor than any of the other symptoms - though the amount of variance explained by symptom changes in general was small.

sources.title: Probability of Transition to Psychosis in Individuals at Clinical High Risk: An Updated Meta-analysis.
sources.finding: Updated meta-analysis: ~19% of clinical-high-risk individuals transition to psychosis by 2 years and ~25% by 3 years — the prodrome/at-risk state.

numbers.what_it_is: cumulative transition-to-psychosis risk in CHR-P at 3 years
numbers.abstract_sentence: In this meta-analysis, 25% of individuals at CHR-P developed psychosis within 3 years.

sources.title: Quantifying heterogeneity attributable to polythetic diagnostic criteria: theoretical framework and empirical application.
sources.finding: Combinatorial + empirical analysis: two people with the same diagnosis can share no symptoms; any two symptom combinations share on average less than half their symptoms.

numbers.what_it_is: symptom overlap between two patients with the same diagnosis under polythetic criteria
numbers.abstract_sentence: in most categories, 2 individuals with the same diagnosis may share no symptoms in common, and that any 2 theoretically possible symptom combinations will share on average less than half their symptoms.

sources.title: Prevalence and classification of hallucinations in multiple sensory modalities in schizophrenia spectrum disorders.
sources.finding: GROUP-cohort study: auditory hallucinations occur in 60-80% of patients, but hallucinations are frequently multimodal (53%) rather than auditory-only (27% unimodal).

numbers.what_it_is: proportion of schizophrenia-spectrum patients with auditory hallucinations
numbers.abstract_sentence: Auditory hallucinations are experienced by 60-80% of all patients diagnosed with a schizophrenia spectrum disorder.

numbers.what_it_is: hallucinations in multiple modalities vs single
numbers.abstract_sentence: 27% of the participants reported unimodal hallucinations and 53% MMHs.

sources.title: Lack of insight among outpatients with schizophrenia.
sources.finding: Outpatient study (n=87): ~half (49.5%) had at least moderately impaired insight and 25% severe deficits — insight is variably impaired along a spectrum, not simply absent.

numbers.what_it_is: proportion of stable outpatients with at least moderately impaired insight
numbers.abstract_sentence: The illness insight of 43 subjects, or 49.5 percent, was at least moderately impaired.
Twenty-one subjects, or 25 percent, had severe insight deficits.
